tasks.title: Transfer Your Driver's License
tasks.what: Update your driver's license with your new state's DMV within the required window.
tasks.why: Driving on an out-of-state license past your state's deadline is a traffic violation and can affect insurance claims.
steps: Look up your new state's DMV deadline — ranges from 10 days (Georgia) to 90 days (California, Texas).
Gather: proof of identity (passport or birth certificate), Social Security card, two proofs of new address, current license.
Visit usa.gov/motor-vehicle-services to schedule a DMV appointment.
Pay the license fee ($10–$89 depending on state) and surrender your old license if required.
Update accounts tied to your ID (TSA PreCheck, professional licenses).

terms.def: Federally compliant driver's license required for domestic flights and federal buildings since May 2025.
Your permanent legal home state, determining which state's DMV has jurisdiction.

tasks.contacts: Your new state's DMV • usa.gov/motor-vehicle-services • REAL ID info: dhs.gov/real-id
mistakes: Waiting for mail before visiting the DMV — the clock starts when you move in.
Assuming your old license is valid indefinitely.
Forgetting to update professional or commercial licenses separately.

tasks.title: Register Your Vehicle and Transfer the Title
tasks.what: Register your car in your new state and transfer the title.
tasks.why: Driving an out-of-state registered vehicle past the deadline can result in fines, and your insurance may deny claims if your registration doesn't match.
steps: Check your new state's deadline — most require registration within 30–90 days.
Schedule a vehicle inspection or emissions test if required by your new state.
Visit the DMV with your title, proof of insurance, driver's license, and odometer statement.
Pay registration fees and any applicable use tax (can range from $0 to over $1,000).
Notify your auto insurer of the new garaging address before registration.

terms.def: The legal process of changing vehicle ownership records from one state to another.
A tax some states charge when you bring a vehicle purchased in another state into their jurisdiction.

tasks.contacts: Your new state's DMV • Your auto lender's title department • Your auto insurance agent
mistakes: Not updating your insurance garaging address first — insurers can deny claims.
Missing the emissions inspection requirement.
Assuming title transfer is automatic with a lien.

tasks.title: Update Voter Registration
tasks.what: Register to vote at your new address.
tasks.why: Voting at your old address after moving is illegal in most states.
steps: Visit vote.gov to register online or find your state's portal.
Check your new state's registration deadline — some allow same-day, others require 15–30 days before an election.
Confirm registration was accepted at vote.gov/verify-voter-registration.
Update registration if you move within the same state as well.

terms.def: Some states automatically register eligible citizens when they interact with the DMV.

tasks.contacts: vote.gov • Your county election office • 1-866-OUR-VOTE for assistance
mistakes: Assuming registration transferred with your license update — only AVR states link these.
Missing the registration deadline before an upcoming election.

tasks.title: Update Will, Power of Attorney, and Legal Documents
tasks.what: Review and update estate planning documents to ensure they comply with your new state's laws.
tasks.why: Estate documents are governed by state law; a will or POA valid in one state may not comply with another's requirements.
steps: Pull out your existing will, power of attorney, healthcare proxy, and advance directive.
Consult a licensed attorney in your new state (find one at americanbar.org).
Ask if your documents meet your new state's execution requirements (witnesses, notarization).
Re-execute documents that need updates with proper witnessing in your new state.
Update beneficiary designations on life insurance, retirement accounts, and bank accounts separately.

terms.def: Legal document authorizing someone to manage your financial affairs, remaining valid if you become incapacitated.
Document specifying your healthcare wishes if you cannot communicate them.

tasks.contacts: State bar referral: americanbar.org • Your current estate planning attorney
mistakes: Assuming documents from your old state are fully valid without review.
Forgetting to update beneficiary designations, which override your will.

tasks.title: File Part-Year Resident Tax Returns in Two States
tasks.what: When moving mid-year, you typically owe income taxes in both states as a part-year resident.
tasks.why: Failing to file in both states can result in penalties and back taxes; some states aggressively audit movers.
steps: Determine your exact move date — this is when you established domicile in the new state.
Update your employer's payroll withholding to the new state immediately.
File a part-year resident return in your old state for income earned while you lived there.
File a part-year resident return in your new state for income earned after your move.
If working remotely, research whether your old state has a "convenience of the employer" rule (NY, CT, DE).
Keep documentation of your move date: lease, utility start dates, moving receipts.

terms.def: Tax filing status for someone who lived in a state for only part of the year due to moving.
Rule used by NY and some states to tax remote workers on all income if remote work was not employer-required.

tasks.contacts: Your state's department of revenue • IRS.gov • Tax software: turbotax.com, hrblock.com
mistakes: Only filing in one state — states share data through the Multi-State Tax Commission.
Not updating payroll withholding mid-year.
Ignoring convenience-of-the-employer rules for remote work.

tasks.title: Update Financial Accounts and Insurance
tasks.what: Update your address across all financial institutions and insurance policies.
tasks.why: Outdated addresses cause missed tax forms, lapsed coverage, and insurance claims denied for wrong-address policies.
steps: Update every financial account: checking, savings, credit cards, investments, 401(k), IRA, HSA.
Contact your health insurer — moving triggers a Special Enrollment Period (60 days) for plan changes.
Update auto insurance garaging address immediately — rates change by location.
Update or purchase homeowner's/renter's insurance for your new property.
Update your address with SSA at ssa.gov/myaccount if applicable.
Shop auto and home insurance rates — they vary significantly by state.

terms.def: A window outside open enrollment for health plan changes, triggered by moving to a new state.
Where your vehicle is primarily kept overnight — determines auto insurance rates.

tasks.contacts: healthcare.gov for plan updates • SSA: 1-800-772-1213 • Your insurance agents
mistakes: Assuming bank accounts update with USPS forwarding — they require direct notification.
Forgetting to update HSA/FSA administrator.
Not shopping insurance rates after the move.

tasks.title: Break Your Lease or Manage Rental Exit
tasks.what: Understand your lease termination rights and obligations before moving.
tasks.why: Breaking a lease improperly can cost months of rent as penalty, damage your rental history, and result in collections.
steps: Read your lease and locate the early termination clause — many allow exit with 30–60 days notice plus 1–2 months' rent.
Check for legal protections: military (SCRA), domestic violence, or uninhabitable conditions.
Notify your landlord in writing via certified mail or email with read receipt.
Research your state's landlord duty-to-mitigate law — most require reasonable re-renting efforts.
Document the unit's condition with timestamped photos before returning keys.
Request your security deposit back in writing — most states require return within 14–30 days.

terms.def: Legal obligation requiring landlords to make reasonable re-renting efforts, reducing your financial liability.
Servicemembers Civil Relief Act — allows military personnel to break a lease without penalty with deployment orders.

tasks.contacts: Local tenant rights organization • HUD: hud.gov/topics/rental_assistance • Your state attorney general
mistakes: Stopping rent without a formal agreement — triggers eviction and credit damage.
Not getting written acknowledgment of your notice.
Failing to document unit condition at move-out.

tasks.title: Set Up Utilities, Mail Forwarding, and Internet
tasks.what: Establish essential services at your new address and redirect mail.
tasks.why: Gaps in service cause missed bills, bounced payments, and lost tax documents.
steps: File USPS Change of Address 1–2 weeks before moving at moversguide.usps.com ($1.10 fee). Forwarding lasts 12 months.
Contact new utility providers 2–4 weeks before move-in for electricity, gas, and water.
Research internet providers using allconnect.com — schedule installation 1–2 weeks ahead.
Update your address directly with important senders (IRS, banks, employer) — USPS forwarding is a backup.
Set up USPS Informed Delivery at informeddelivery.usps.com for digital mail previews.

terms.def: USPS service redirecting first-class mail for up to 12 months (magazines: 60 days).

tasks.contacts: USPS: moversguide.usps.com • allconnect.com for internet • Your new city's utilities department
mistakes: Relying only on USPS forwarding for important mail.
Scheduling internet for move-in day with a 1–2 week backlog.
Forgetting to cancel utilities at your old address.

script.intro: When calling to set up or transfer utilities at your new address:
lines: "Hi, I'm moving to [new address] and I need to start [electric/gas/water] service. My move-in date is [date]. Can you set up a new account and schedule the service to begin that day?"
"I also need to cancel service at my current address, [old address], effective [date]. Can you do both on this call? And is there a deposit required for the new account?"
script.note: Call 2–4 weeks before moving. Ask about deposits (often waived if you transfer an existing account). Get confirmation numbers for both the start and stop of service.

tasks.title: Transfer Medical Records and Find New Providers
tasks.what: Request medical records and establish care with new providers before a health need arises.
tasks.why: Waiting until you're sick can mean months-long waits for new patient appointments.
steps: Contact each provider and request complete records. Under HIPAA, they must fulfill within 30 days.
Request records in electronic format for easy sharing.
Get a 90-day supply of maintenance medications before moving.
Find new providers using your insurance network directory, Zocdoc, or Healthgrades.
If you have a chronic condition, ask your current provider for a care transition letter.
Register with a new pharmacy and transfer prescription history.

terms.def: Federal law giving patients the right to access and obtain copies of their medical records.
Summary from a departing provider documenting diagnoses, medications, and recommendations for new providers.

tasks.contacts: Zocdoc: zocdoc.com • Healthgrades: healthgrades.com • SAMHSA mental health locator: findtreatment.gov
mistakes: Waiting until you have a health crisis — new patient appointments have 4–12 week waits.
Not getting a 90-day prescription supply before moving.
Assuming records transfer automatically.

tasks.title: Enroll Children in New Schools
tasks.what: Research, select, and enroll your children, transferring academic and medical records in advance.
tasks.why: Delayed enrollment disrupts learning, and IEP/504 services must legally resume within specific timeframes.
steps: Research school districts 4–6 weeks before using greatschools.org.
Contact the new school for enrollment requirements: immunization records, birth certificate, proof of address, transcripts.
Request official records from your child's current school in writing.
If your child has an IEP or 504 Plan, notify the new school in writing before enrollment. They must provide comparable services from day one.
Confirm your new state's immunization requirements at cdc.gov/vaccines/imz-managers.
Visit the school before the first day if possible.

terms.def: Individualized Education Program — legally binding document outlining special education services for students with disabilities.
Accommodations plan for students with disabilities who need support but don't qualify for special education.

tasks.contacts: GreatSchools: greatschools.org • New school district special ed coordinator • Wrightslaw: wrightslaw.com
mistakes: Waiting until after the move to contact the school.
Assuming IEP services transfer seamlessly — proactive written notification is required.
Not verifying immunization requirements in the new state.

tasks.title: Notify Employer and Update Payroll
tasks.what: Inform your employer, update state tax withholding, and handle remote work tax implications.
tasks.why: Incorrect withholding creates tax liability, and your employer may not be registered to employ workers in your new state.
steps: Notify HR in writing with your confirmed move date.
Submit a new state withholding certificate so taxes are withheld correctly.
Confirm your employer is registered in your new state — this can take 4–12 weeks if not.
If working remotely, ask about convenience-of-the-employer rules (NY, CT, DE, NE, PA).
Update your address in all employer systems: payroll, benefits, emergency contacts.
Review your employment agreement for geographic restrictions or noncompete clauses.

terms.def: State tax form directing your employer how much state income tax to withhold from each paycheck.
A connection between a business and a state requiring compliance with that state's tax and employment laws.

tasks.contacts: Your HR or People Operations team • Your state's department of labor
mistakes: Not telling your employer until after the move — creates weeks of incorrect withholding.
Assuming your employer is registered in your new state.
Not reviewing noncompete agreements before relocating.
